message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 clinical presentation ranges from asymptomatic to fatal outcome. This variability is due in part to host genome specific mutations. Recently, two families in which COVID-19 segregates like an X-linked recessive monogenic disorder environmentally conditioned by SARS-CoV-2 have been reported leading to identification of loss-of-function variants in
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We sought to determine whether the two families represent the tip of the iceberg of a subset of COVID-19 male patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We compared male subjects with extreme phenotype selected from the Italian GEN-COVID cohort of 1178 SARS-CoV-2-infected subjects (<60y, 79 severe cases versus 77 control cases). We applied the LASSO Logistic Regression analysis, considering only rare variants on the young male subset, picking up
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    as the most important susceptibility gene.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Rare
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    missense variants were predicted to impact on protein function in severely affected males and in none of the asymptomatic subjects. We then investigated a similar white European cohort in Spain, confirming the impact of
                    <jats:italic>TRL7</jats:italic>
                    variants. A gene expression profile analysis in peripheral blood mononuclear cells after stimulation with TLR7 agonist demonstrated a reduction of mRNA level of TLR7, IRF7, ISG15, IFN-□ and IFN-γ in COVID-19 patients compared with unaffected controls demonstrating an impairment in type I and II INF responses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Young males with
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    loss-of-function mutations and severe COVID-19 in the two reported families represent only a fraction of a broader and complex host genome situation. Specifically, missense mutations in the X-linked recessive
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    disorder may significantly contribute to disease susceptibility in up to 4% of severe COVID-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Clinical Implication</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this new yet complex scenario, our observations provide the basis for a personalized interferon-based therapy in patients with rare
                    <jats:italic>TLR7</jats:italic>
                    variants.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CAPSULE SUMMARY</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our results in large cohorts from Italy and Spain showed that X-linked recessive TLR7 disorder may represent the cause of disease susceptibility to COVID-19 in up to 4% of severely affected young male cases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Association of Toll-like receptor 7 variants with life-threatening COVID-19 disease in males

reference.article-title: Hepatitis C virus inhibits CD4 T cell function via binding to Toll-like receptor 7

reference.unstructured: Daga S , Fallerini C , Baldassarri M , Fava F , Valentino F , Doddato G , et al. Employing a Systematic Approach to Biobanking and Analyzing Genetic and Clinical Data for Advancing COVID-19 Research. medRxiv 2020.07.24.20161307
